work: Zaliznyak, Конспект санскритской грамматики (Sanskrit Grammar Synopsis), 2004
synthesis: Two-claim seed — deliberately small, not padded. Konspekt turned out to be the hardest of the four sources checked so far (Kochergina, Bühler, Knauer, Ocherk) to verify with existing ground truth, for two distinct reasons found by actually reading all 657 lines rather than assuming from the earlier genre-check grep: (1) it is overwhelmingly tabular/mechanical (declension paradigms, conjugation paradigms, a root-and-preverb lexicon) with only a thin vein of hedged prose running through it, and (2) several of that thin vein's richest claims are Rigveda-specific exact fractions (1/3, 7/8, 3/5, 1/2, 5/6 of cases) that need period-isolated corpus data this pipeline's DCS ground truth does not currently expose — confirmed by checking, not assumed. KZ-1 (the seṭ/aniṭ/veṭ classification is genuinely mixed, "many fluctuations and inconsistencies") is TRUE by cross-reference to Kochergina's already-established HK-4 finding (future-stem seṭ share 56.8%, i.e. close to a 50/50 split, not a clean rule) — a different book independently confirms the same underlying messiness Zaliznyak describes qualitatively. KZ-2 (periphrastic future middle voice is "редко", rare) is TRUE but weakly evidenced: DCS-2021's own tense/mood codebook has no separate tag for medium periphrastic future at all (only "Periph. Future" / Active, 1,290 tokens) — consistent with rarity, though the absence of a corpus tag is not the same as a measured ratio, and this is flagged honestly rather than overclaimed as a precise number. No errors found; the remaining 15 candidates are genuinely open, not a reluctant-to-verify backlog — five specifically need new corpus infrastructure (Rigveda-period isolation) before anyone can promote them.

claims.claim_ru: (В индийской традиции корни, получающие вставное i, называются seṭ, не получающие --- aniṭ, имеющие оба варианта во всех таких формах --- veṭ.) В данном пункте имеется, однако, много колебаний и непоследовательностей.
claims.number: future-stem seṭ (-iṣya) share = 56.8% of distinct future forms (Kochergina HK-4) — close to an even split, not a clean majority/minority rule, consistent with Zaliznyak's 'many fluctuations and inconsistencies' characterization
claims.ref: DCS-2021 (KocherginaUchebnik_1998/verify_claims_dcs.py HK4_future); cf. KocherginaUchebnik_1998 HK-4, BuhlerLeitfaden_1923 HB-59 (same corpus fact independently confirmed twice already)
claims.note: Third independent confirmation (after Kochergina and Bühler) that the seṭ/aniṭ split in the future stem is genuinely close to even rather than following the clean root-class correlation a learner might expect — Zaliznyak's hedge here is honest, not an undersell or oversell.

claims.claim_ru: Футурум 2 (описательный футурум): имя деятеля на -tar- (в N) + связка as 'быть' (кроме 3-го лица, где без связки)... Med. (редко): dātāhe (), dātāse, dātā, dātāsmahe, dātādhve, dātāras, dātāsvahe, dātāsāthe, dātārau.
claims.number: DCS-2021's tense/mood codebook (timws.csv, 42 codes) has no separate tag for medium periphrastic future at all — only code 7 'PPF. Future Active' (1,290 tokens = 0.17% of verbal) is tracked; no corresponding medium-voice code exists in the full code list (verify_claims_dcs.py KZ2_periphrastic_future_codebook)
claims.note: Weakly evidenced by design, flagged honestly: the corpus's own tagging scheme carrying no medium-voice periphrastic-future code is consistent with genuine rarity, but an absent tag is not the same as a measured token ratio — this is the best available evidence, not a precise confirmation. Cf. ZalizniakOcherk_1978 §159 (Sonnet 5's own earlier harvest, unverified there too) — the same claim about the same paradigm cell appears independently in both Zaliznyak texts, neither yet given a hard number.
